perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws in force in the Province of Ontario (excluding any conflict of laws rule or principle which might refer such construction to the laws of another jurisdiction). The Parties hereto agree to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Ontario and waive any objection relating to improper venue or forum non conveniens to the conduct of any proceeding in any such court.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws in force in the Province of Ontario (excluding any conflict of laws rule or principle which might refer such construction to the laws of another jurisdiction). The Parties hereto agree to submit to the jurisdiction of the courts of the Province of Ontario.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of 'exclusive jurisdiction' and the waiver of objection relating to improper venue or forum non conveniens weakens consumer rights regarding where they can file a lawsuit, potentially violating consumer protection laws that ensure fair access to legal recourse, especially if CHT tries to force arbitration or out-of-province legal proceedings.
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Sch. A, s. 7(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 7(1) of the Ontario Consumer Protection Act states that the Act applies even if the agreement purports to be governed by the laws of another jurisdiction. By removing the section that stipulates exclusive jurisdiction and waiving objections to venue, the agreement could be interpreted as an attempt to circumvent Ontario's consumer protection laws, which would be a direct contradiction. The modification makes it harder for a consumer to enforce their rights under Ontario law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specified 'exclusive jurisdiction' and waived objections to venue, ensuring disputes would be resolved in Ontario courts. The changed text removes these protections, potentially allowing the company to force consumers into arbitration or out-of-province legal proceedings, contrary to consumer protection laws. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Consumer Protection Act, 2002, suggesting the importance of consumer rights and the Act's application, implying the modification could be an attempt to circumvent Ontario's consumer protection laws as explained in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Both Parties shall, at all times during the currency of this Agreement and for a period of one (1) year after the termination or expiration of this Agreement, maintain the following policies of insurance in effect: (i) a comprehensive general liability insurance policy, with minimum coverage of $1,000,000 per occurrence and in the annual aggregate for product liability and completed operations, covering bodily and personal injury, including death, and property damage, including loss of use; and (ii) an information and network technology blended liability insurance policy with an insured limit of at least $1,000,000 in the aggregate.
perturbation.changed_text: Both Parties shall, at all times during the currency of this Agreement, maintain the following policies of insurance in effect: (i) a general liability insurance policy covering bodily and personal injury and property damage; and (ii) a network technology liability insurance policy.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'minimum coverage' and the 'one year after termination' requirements removes consumer protections. Ontario law may require specific minimum coverage amounts for certain types of commercial activities, particularly those involving data and technology services. Removing these specifications potentially leaves consumers vulnerable if liabilities exceed the (now unspecified) coverage, or if a claim arises after the agreement expires.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Insurance Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. I.8, s. 234
perturbation.law_citation: Insurance Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. I.8, s. 234
perturbation.law_explanation: While section 234 of the Insurance Act doesn't specify a minimum amount, it allows the Financial Services Regulatory Authority of Ontario (FSRA) to establish requirements concerning insurance, including minimum coverage. This modification creates the potential for CHT to maintain insurance policies that do not meet minimum standards as defined and required by Ontario law. While not directly specifying an amount, the Insurance Act allows regulatory bodies to define what is adequate. This omission therefore contradicts those regulatory standards. Removing insurance coverage post-agreement termination would also violate consumer rights.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the 'minimum coverage' and 'one year after termination' requirements. The Law Explanation states that the Insurance Act allows regulatory bodies to define what is adequate, so removing this could violate those regulatory standards. The Perturbation Explanation also states that removing these specifications potentially leaves consumers vulnerable if liabilities exceed the (now unspecified) coverage, or if a claim arises after the agreement expires. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references the Insurance Act R.S.O. 1990, CHAPTER I.8, which is the act in question.

perturbation.original_text: CHT shall require each End User to enter into and execute a subscription agreement in a form and substance satisfactory to Ehave and CHT, acting reasonably, and which contains provisions similar to Ehave's collaboration agreement for the use of the Ehave solution (the "Subscription Agreement"). Ehave shall be a third party beneficiary hereunder, but shall not have any obligations to the End User thereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: CHT shall require each End User to enter into and execute a subscription agreement (the "Subscription Agreement").
perturbation.explanation: Removing the requirement that the Subscription Agreement contains provisions similar to Ehave's collaboration agreement for the use of the Ehave solution weakens consumer protection by allowing CHT to create subscription agreements that may not adequately protect end users. Ontario's consumer protection laws mandate certain disclosures and protections, which might be absent if the 'similarity' clause is removed, and if Ehave is no longer acting 'reasonably' in regards to the Subscription Agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Sch. A, s. 4
perturbation.law_citation: Consumer Protection Act, 2002, S.O. 2002, c. 30, Sch. A, s. 4
perturbation.law_explanation: Section 4 of the Ontario Consumer Protection Act states that a consumer agreement must comply with the requirements of the Act and its regulations. By removing the requirement that the subscription agreement contain provisions similar to Ehave's collaboration agreement (which presumably includes consumer protections), there is a higher risk that the subscription agreement will not comply with the Consumer Protection Act. The addition of the clause 'acting reasonably' adds protection and compliance. Its removal is a contradiction.

perturbation.justification: The original text mandates that the subscription agreement contains provisions similar to Ehave's collaboration agreement, which implies a baseline level of consumer protection. The modified text removes this requirement. The Law Explanation says that by removing the requirement that the subscription agreement contain provisions similar to Ehave's collaboration agreement (which presumably includes consumer protections), there is a higher risk that the subscription agreement will not comply with the Consumer Protection Act. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, where removing essential information can make a contract non-compliant, specifically by omitting a legally mandated consumer protection.
